paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 08: The Window (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary Four girl saying goodbye to her bedroom window on the morning her family moves out of the three-room HDB flat they have lived in for eighteen years, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [19 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect). Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2] as in the real paper: 10 short-answer questions (Q1-Q10) — 2 literal, 3 vocabulary-in-context, 3 inference, 2 language-for-effect — plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q11). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
The flat was almost empty. Eighteen years of furniture had been carried down to the lorry by the movers in less than three hours. My mother stood in the kitchen, holding the last cup of coffee she would ever make in this flat. My father was checking the bedrooms one final time. He found nothing left behind. He never did.

Paragraph 2
I walked through the rooms slowly. Without furniture, they looked smaller than I remembered. The living room where my brother and I had once built a fort of cushions felt the size of a single mattress. The dining room where my mother had sliced ang ku kueh into halves for school recess felt no bigger than the dining table that had filled it.

Paragraph 3
My bedroom was last. I stood at the window — the same window where I had done every page of homework from Primary 1 to Sec 4. The desk had been beneath it, the chair pulled close. I had watched the lights of the MRT come on at night through this glass. I had watched the full moon rise over the carpark every Mid-Autumn from this exact angle.

Paragraph 4
I thought of specific things, the way you do at moments like these. The time I had stayed up till three in the morning studying for the PSLE, and my father had come to the door and said, very quietly, 'You don't have to push so hard.' The time I had seen a kingfisher land on the air-conditioning compressor outside the window, electric blue against the grey of the wall. The time my brother had thrown a paper aeroplane from inside the room, and we had watched it spiral down four storeys before it landed on someone's umbrella.

Paragraph 5
From the corridor I heard my father. 'Ah Niang, time to go.' I did not answer at once. I needed a moment more.

Paragraph 6
I pressed my palm flat against the window. The glass was cool against my skin. With my fingertip I traced the line of the MRT track in the distance — the same line I would never trace again from here. I held my hand there for a long moment. Then I lifted it. The print of my palm was left behind for a few seconds, fading, before the glass forgot me entirely.

Paragraph 7
Downstairs, the movers shouted up the stairwell — last box, ready. I joined my mother in the lift. She did not say anything. She knew what the rooms had looked like for the last time too. The lift doors closed.

Paragraph 8
As the lift descended, I imagined for a moment the silhouette of my old window passing in reverse through the closed lift doors — first the sixth floor, then the fifth, then the fourth, then gone. I held my mother's hand in the corner of the lift. She squeezed it once. By the time we reached the ground floor, I had already begun to miss it. But I was ready.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. PSLE: Primary School Leaving Examination; ang ku kueh: traditional Chinese steamed cake; HDB: Housing & Development Board, Singapore's public housing.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 1, how long had the family lived in the flat? [1]
Q2. From Paragraph 3, from which year to which year had the narrator done her homework at the window? [1]
Q3. What does the word 'traced' in 'With my fingertip I traced the line of the MRT track' (Paragraph 6) suggest about how the narrator moved her finger? [1]
questions.answer: slowly and carefully following its shape

questions.stem: Q4. What does the word 'spiral' in 'we had watched it spiral down four storeys' (Paragraph 4) suggest about how the paper aeroplane fell? [1]
Q5. What does the phrase 'the glass forgot me entirely' (Paragraph 6) suggest about the trace the narrator left on the window? [1]
questions.answer: it vanished completely / left no lasting mark

questions.stem: Q6. Using your own words, explain why the narrator's father said 'You don't have to push so hard' (Paragraph 4). [2]
questions.answer: ① he saw she had stayed up until three in the morning studying for the PSLE; ② he was worried about her, valuing her well-being above the exam result

questions.stem: Q7. Using your own words, explain why the narrator did not answer her father at once when he called 'Ah Niang, time to go' (Paragraph 5). [2]
questions.answer: ① she needed a little more time to say a final goodbye to the room; ② answering straight away would have meant leaving without that last pause

questions.stem: Q8. Using your own words, explain why the narrator says her mother 'knew what the rooms had looked like for the last time too' (Paragraph 7). [2]
questions.answer: ① her mother was also leaving the home she had made for the family; ② she shared the same silent goodbye, feeling the loss just as the narrator did

questions.stem: Q9. What is the effect of 'He found nothing left behind. He never did.' (Paragraph 1)? [2]
questions.answer: the short, blunt sentences show the father is thorough and dependable — he always checks carefully so nothing is forgotten, adding a quiet note of admiration

questions.stem: Q10. What is the effect of 'the print of my palm was left behind for a few seconds, fading, before the glass forgot me entirely' (Paragraph 6)? [2]
questions.answer: it shows how briefly we leave a mark on a place — even a physical print fades fast, working as a symbol for the move itself, when the home soon 'forgets' her

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling changes as she walks through the empty flat for the last time. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: reluctant / wistful / aching / accepting / triumphant / numb / anxious / restless. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Window' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift as she walks through the flat one last time — from wistful as she compares the bare empty rooms to her larger memories of them, to aching as the vivid specific memories of her childhood come back to her, to reluctant as she presses her palm to the window and cannot quite lift it away, ending accepting as her mother squeezes her hand once in the descending lift. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 2-3, walking through empty rooms that look smaller than she remembered and standing at her bedroom window where she had done every page of homework? [1]
Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 4, vividly remembering the PSLE night, the electric-blue kingfisher and the paper aeroplane spiralling down four storeys? [1]
Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 6, pressing her palm flat against the cool window and holding it there for a long moment before she can lift it away? [1]

Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8, holding her mother's hand in the descending lift and concluding that she had already begun to miss it but she was ready? [1]
